🟡 ReGenerOsa Collective

Case Study
Y

Tags
33314415-10e1-8166-b8a7-ffb8c528af98, 33314415-10e1-81a3-a4c8-d5e5948fe526, 33314415-10e1-81b0-bf6e-fa361478dbf2, 33314415-10e1-817d-af1e-fe81df7ef31c, 33314415-10e1-8163-bf4c-e6a952135f8c, 33314415-10e1-8168-8959-d5e2a5457fc6, 33314415-10e1-8111-a00a-c80e1492315c, 33314415-10e1-81f5-83c4-c99618834d4e, 33314415-10e1-811f-a2eb-c276ab53a2f6, 33314415-10e1-81b0-9adb-d91296317ceb

33314415-10e1-8148-b115-f145e982e3c3, 33314415-10e1-81c0-9ba1-fa35660d65d1, 33314415-10e1-814b-b2b3-cfd941f548c8, 33314415-10e1-8175-a791-d94a755bcf4e, 33314415-10e1-81e1-8b3f-da5fe5484336, 33314415-10e1-8164-aaf3-c3e5ec59afd4
network_affiliation
[[BioFi Project]]
background_section
## Background The Osa Peninsula in southern Costa Rica sits where Pacific coastline meets tropical rainforest. It is often called the [most biologically intense place on Earth](https://youtu.be/nowoYmBRijw). Less than one-thousandth of the planet’s land mass holds [about 2.5% of global biodiversity](https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o)—rainforest, mangroves, and wildlife at a scale that draws worldwide attention. That richness sits alongside deep strain. Globalized economic pressures erode social cohesion, intergenerational knowledge, and collective capacity. Roughly [40–70% of the local economy is informal](https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o), and [about 80% depends on tourism](https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o). After gold mining and logging, the bioregion now faces both conservation-driven exclusion and tourism-driven development. [Substantial philanthropic funding reaches the Osa](https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o), yet most of it flows to conservation. Work is often fragmented and siloed, with missed chances to collaborate. Local communities are sidelined from funding and decisions; strategies lean on outside academics and scientists. Well-meaning regenerative projects from elsewhere can land as imposed fixes rather than support for people who already know the land. ### Origins and Network Trisha Stapleton is a [23-year resident of the Osa Peninsula](https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o) and, from the local Cabécar population, has unlearned assumptions she once held and deepened how she relates to the land. Around February 2020 this work took clearer shape. She looked online for what “regeneration” meant and found the Earth Regenerators Network—a mix of theory and on-the-ground practice. The Earth Regenerators Fund was forming; ReGenerOsa received support from its first round of regeneration funding. From there came the RegenerOsa Collective: how to bring trust-based funding that supports local communities into a trust-based process on the ground?
Sources
33314415-10e1-81c0-a089-d94fa5e7f319, 33314415-10e1-81c5-b678-d3bda0742e07
patterns

Place
Corcovado National Park, Costa Rica
place
Osa Peninsula, Costa Rica
activities
Stone Soup Social|Manos Cambiadas|Food Sovereignty and Seed Saving Networks|ReGenerOsa Women's Governance Circle|Osa Biocultural Fund|Osa Regenerative Learning Center (El Local)
description
ReGenerOsa is a biocultural regeneration collective led by women who are reclaiming land, language, and local governance in the face of rapid ecological degradation, cultural loss, and speculative tourism.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Over the past five years, their initiatives have held an open funding round reaching [nine of twelve local communities](https://youtu.be/nowoYmBRijw), run the farmers market for more than four years, mobilized 1,200-plus volunteers through Manos Cambiadas, convened 30-plus bioregional stakeholder initiatives, and piloted circular economy practices, community currencies, and regenerative techniques. The long-term aim is to co-create a municipal strategy for a fully regenerative canton—showing how a whole region can organize around ecological and social health. Many systems operate across the Osa without a shared center; little ties them together. ReGenerOsa seeks to be that weaving force—aligning elected bodies, institutions, investors, and community groups around a shared sense of where the region goes next.
website
https://abundantearthfoundation.org/osa/
bioregional_context
The Osa Peninsula is one of the most biodiverse places on Earth, holding 2.5% of Earth's biodiversity on less than one-thousandth of the planet's land mass. The region faces dual pressures of conservation-driven exclusion and tourism-driven development.
category
Bioregional Organizer
